definition: Coffee species producing ~70% of global production. Narrow climate requirements: 15–24°C, 1,500–2,000mm rainfall, 800–2,000m elevation, no frost tolerance.
Coffee species producing ~30% of global production. More tolerant of heat and disease than Arabica; grown at 200–800m elevation.
Equatorial band (25°N to 30°S latitude) where coffee grows commercially. Covers 81 producing countries across three continents.
French term for how geography, climate, and soil express themselves in a crop's flavor. Applied to coffee, wine, tea, chocolate.
Growing coffee under shade-tree canopy. Supports biodiversity, soil health, and climate resilience.
Amharic word meaning both 'coffee' and 'the Ethiopian coffee ceremony'. The ceremony has three rounds: abol, tona, baraka.
First round of the Ethiopian Buna ceremony. Strongest brew; narratively represents initial encounter or rising action.
Second round of the Ethiopian Buna ceremony. Medium strength; represents deepening relationship or development.
Third round of the Ethiopian Buna ceremony. Lightest brew; represents blessing, departure, and peace. Also means 'blessing' in Amharic.
Arabic word for coffee. Originally meant 'wine'. Traveled through Turkish ('kahve'), Italian ('caffè'), and into English.
Yemeni port city where coffee first entered international trade. Gave the world the word 'mocha'.
Islamic mystical tradition. 15th-century Sufi monks in Yemen were among coffee's earliest documented users; used it to sustain long prayer.
Spaces where citizens debate ideas outside government control. Coffeehouses were arguably Europe's first public spheres.
Nickname for 17th–18th century English coffeehouses — one penny admission gave you access to the conversations and debates of the day.
Dutch colonial forced-cultivation system in Java (1696–1870s). Required farmers to dedicate portion of land to export crops including coffee.
How demand for a good changes when its price changes. Coffee has INELASTIC demand — a 70% price increase barely reduced consumption in 2024.
Fraction of revenue that recirculates within the local economy. Local cafés: ~68%. Chains: ~43%. (AMIBA, 2023).
Certification system ensuring minimum prices ($1.60/lb floor + $0.20/lb premium), direct producer payments, and labor standards.
Relationship-based sourcing model where roasters buy directly from farms, often at multi-year commitments and higher prices ($3.50–6.00/lb).
Bulk green coffee traded on futures markets (the 'C-Market'). Price-driven, quality-optional, farmer-exposed to global volatility.
Network of stages moving a product from source to consumer. Coffee: farm → export → import → roaster → retail → cup.
